critic_raw:
Overall this is a carefully designed experiment. The labeling is precise, leakage controls are explicit, and the evaluation metric is operationally motivated. I looked hard for fundamental flaws. Here is what I found:

---

**Issue 1: Stratified random split on a temporal dataset — the core methodological problem**

Claim: Stratifying over prediction months ensures balanced composition and reduces sensitivity to unusual quarters, while keeping all features anchored strictly before each prediction point per-example.

Why it might be wrong: This is a causal/temporal data structure. Customer trajectories persist across months — the same account appears in multiple customer-month prediction points. A stratified random split will routinely place earlier months of a given account in the test set and later months in the training set, or vice versa. This creates two distinct failure modes:

(a) Label leakage via account identity: The model learns account-level patterns in training months and is then evaluated on different months from the same account. Because churn is sticky (an account that almost churned at month T is more likely to churn at month T+6), the model's apparent generalization may be inflated — it has already seen the 'personality' of accounts it is scored on. This is not feature leakage in the strict sense (no future features are used), but it is distributional leakage: the test distribution is not independent of the train distribution at the account level.

(b) Embedding/representation leakage: The transformer fine-tuning on text from account A at time T1 will have seen communication style, product-use vocabulary, and complaint patterns that are then re-encountered when the same account A appears in the test set at time T2. The text signal benefit being measured may therefore reflect memorization of account writing style rather than generalizable language-to-churn signal.

The correct split for this setting is a temporal holdout: train on all prediction points before month M, validate on months M to M+k, test on months M+k+1 onward. This preserves causal ordering and ensures no account's future is predicted using its own past in training. The methodology's stated rationale — 'less sensitive to any single unusual quarter' — is a real concern, but it does not justify abandoning temporal ordering; it can be addressed by averaging across multiple temporal windows.

Evidence for/against: Compare AUC-ROC and precision-at-recall under (a) stratified random split vs. (b) strict temporal split. If (a) materially outperforms (b), leakage from account identity is inflating results. A further diagnostic: measure per-account AUC restricted to accounts that appear exclusively in test (new accounts entering only after the training cutoff) vs. accounts with training-set history. If performance is substantially higher for the latter group, the mechanism is confirmed.

---

**Issue 2: Preprocessing on full corpus before splitting — scaling and vocabulary leakage**

Claim: 'Preprocessing components that do not use labels will be parameterized once using corpus-level statistics from the full extracted feature pool, then frozen.'

Why it might be wrong: This is a well-intentioned claim but conflates 'no label leakage' with 'no leakage.' Scaling parameters (mean, variance) computed on the full corpus include test-set statistics. For tabular features this is a weak effect, but for the tokenization vocabulary and any unsupervised imputation model, the test set's language and value distributions have directly influenced the learned preprocessing. In a production deployment, you would not have access to future data when building your vocabulary or imputation model. The vocabulary built from the full corpus will include terms that appear only in the test period — meaning the transformer's embedding space is calibrated to test-time language in a way that would not be possible in deployment. For a BERT-family model where the tokenizer is essentially fixed from pretraining, the vocabulary issue is minor, but the imputation model and scaling are not.

Evidence for/against: Refit scaling and imputation strictly on the training fold only and compare test-set performance. If performance drops, the preprocessing leakage was contributing. For the vocabulary specifically, check whether any tokens that appear only in the test transcripts account for meaningful model attention weights.

---

**Issue 3: Threshold is set on validation, then frozen and applied to test — but the retraining step invalidates this**

Claim: 'The decision threshold is set on the validation set to achieve 80% recall, and this same threshold is applied to the test set.' Then: 'each model is retrained on train+validation combined.'

Why it might be wrong: The threshold is calibrated to the model trained on train-only. After retraining on train+validation combined, the model's score distribution shifts — there is no guarantee the originally selected threshold still achieves 80% recall on the test set. If the retrained model's scores are systematically higher or lower, the actual recall on test may be 72% or 88%, and the 'precision-at-80%-recall' comparison becomes a comparison at different operating points for each model. This is a standard but real flaw in threshold-then-retrain pipelines.

Evidence for/against: Report actual achieved recall on the test set for each model at the frozen threshold. If either model deviates from 80% recall by more than ~2 percentage points, the primary metric comparison is confounded. The clean fix is to set the threshold on the retrained model's scores over a held-out calibration slice, or to evaluate using interpolated precision-recall curves rather than a single threshold.

---

**Issue 4: Text leakage audit is insufficient in scope**

Claim: A manual audit of 100 random transcripts in the test set verifies no cancellation signal remains after filtering.

Why it might be wrong: 100 transcripts is a spot-check, not a verification. The filtering rule is based on agent-marked cancellation requests or closure summaries, but cancellation intent is routinely expressed in language before it is formally marked: phrases like 'we're evaluating alternatives,' 'our budget has been cut,' or 'we need to discuss our renewal.' These signals are genuine pre-churn language the model should learn, but if they systematically appear within the 2-day exclusion window or are present in chats that were not agent-marked as cancellation-related, the filter fails silently. The audit of 100 samples has low power to detect a 1–5% contamination rate, and 100 samples drawn randomly from a test set of ~7,500 examples (15% of 50K) gives a 95% CI that cannot rule out contamination rates below roughly 3%.

This is less a fatal flaw and more an underpowered control, but it matters precisely because the hypothesis being tested is whether text adds signal — and the most obvious confound is direct cancellation language in the transcript.

Evidence for/against: Audit should be stratified: oversample transcripts from churned accounts, from accounts that churned within 5 days of prediction day N, and from accounts whose predicted churn probability is in the top decile. A keyword/regex scan for cancellation-adjacent language on the full filtered transcript set would be more reliable than a 100-sample manual audit.

---

**Summary**

Issue 1 (temporal split) is the most fundamental: the stratified random split on a repeated-measures temporal dataset undermines the independence assumption required for valid generalization measurement. It is a design-level problem, not a tuning choice. Issue 3 (threshold invalidated by retraining) is a concrete execution flaw that could cause the primary metric comparison to be at mismatched operating points. Issues 2 and 4 are real but smaller in likely magnitude.

The methodology is otherwise well-thought-out. The baseline parity, class imbalance handling, stratified reporting, and feature fairness controls are all appropriate. The issues above are not manufacturing concerns — they have specific failure mechanisms and testable diagnostics.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on repeated-measures temporal data allows account identity leakage: the same customer appears in both train and test at different time points, inflating apparent generalization for both tabular and text models.

Preprocessing (scaling, imputation) is fit on the full corpus including test examples, meaning test-period distributional statistics influence the preprocessing applied to the test set — violating production deployment assumptions.

Decision threshold is calibrated on the train-only model but applied after retraining on train+validation combined, meaning the actual recall achieved on the test set may not be 80%, making the primary metric comparison evaluate models at different operating points.
Manual audit of only 100 transcripts is underpowered to detect low-rate cancellation-language contamination in filtered transcripts, particularly for churned accounts closest to the prediction day.

Stratified random split rather than temporal holdout means the model is evaluated on interpolation within a period rather than forward extrapolation, which is the deployment scenario — and the text-based transformer is differentially susceptible to this because lexical patterns are period-specific.

Preprocessing statistics (scaling, imputation, potentially tokenization vocabulary) derived from the full feature pool including test examples introduce preprocessing leakage, most materially if a domain-specific tokenizer is trained on all transcripts.

The decision threshold is set on validation before retraining on train+val combined; after retraining the score distribution shifts, so the threshold no longer corresponds to 80% recall on the test set, invalidating the primary metric as stated.

Stratified random split across time creates soft temporal leakage because a customer's later observations appear in training while earlier ones appear in test, contaminating learned representations with future customer trajectories.

Preprocessing statistics (scaling, vocabulary, imputation) derived from the full corpus including test data constitutes leakage for evolving support language distributions, even though no labels are used.

The threshold fixed on the validation set to achieve 80% recall will yield a different actual recall on the test set, making the precision-at-80%-recall comparison between models invalid if effective recall levels differ.

The experiment conflates 'does text contain signal beyond tabular features' with 'does the transformer architecture outperform GBT,' requiring a 2x2 ablation (text-only vs. tabular-only vs. combined, for both architectures) to separate these questions.
Auditing only 100 transcripts for post-churn text contamination is statistically underpowered to detect rare but systematic filtering failures in the churned subpopulation.

assessor_results.critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-constructed and addresses many common failure modes. However, I identify three genuine methodological concerns worth examining.

**Issue 1: Random split on temporal data — the core leakage problem**

The methodology uses stratified random sampling by month composition rather than a temporal cut. This means a customer observed in month T can appear in the training set while a *different* observation of the same customer in month T+6 appears in the test set, or vice versa. More critically, customers who churn in month T+3 provide implicit signal about the operating environment, billing patterns, and support language of the period. Because the split is cross-sectional rather than temporal, the model sees future-period data during training and is evaluated on past-period data (or vice versa). In production, the model will only ever be trained on data from before its deployment date and scored on future data it has never seen. The stratified random split measures within-distribution interpolation; the production task is forward extrapolation.

The specific mechanism of failure: if churn propensity is non-stationary (seasonal renewal cycles, pricing changes, product releases, economic conditions), the model trained on randomly sampled months — which includes samples drawn from periods after the test-set months — will capture dynamics that are unavailable in a true forward-looking setting. The text signal in particular (support language shifts as product evolves) is especially susceptible because BERT fine-tuning can anchor to period-specific lexical patterns. A temporal holdout (e.g., last 3–4 months as test, preceding months as train/val) would directly test the deployment-relevant scenario.

Evidence for or against: Compare AUC-ROC and precision-at-recall between the stratified-random split and a strict temporal holdout on the same data. If performance drops materially under the temporal split — especially more for the transformer than for the gradient-boosted model — this confirms the concern. If both degrade proportionally, the relative comparison may still be valid, but the absolute performance estimates will be inflated.

**Issue 2: Preprocessing leakage via corpus-level statistics derived from the full feature pool**

The methodology states that scaling, tokenization vocabulary, and unsupervised imputation are computed on the 'full extracted feature pool' and then frozen. If the full pool includes test-set examples, this constitutes preprocessing leakage. Specifically: (a) continuous-feature scaling absorbs test-set range and distribution into the normalization, giving the model implicit knowledge of the test marginal distribution; (b) tokenization vocabulary built on all transcripts — including test-set transcripts — allows the model to 'see' test-period terminology during embedding, even if the labels are withheld; (c) missing-value imputation means fill values encode statistics partly from test accounts.

For tabular features, this is usually a minor concern because range information leaks very little about labels. For the transformer specifically, this matters more: if the tokenizer's vocabulary or subword merges are constructed over test-period support chat transcripts, the model's embedding space is shaped by future language patterns. In practice, pre-trained BERT tokenizers use fixed WordPiece vocabularies, so if the team uses a standard pre-trained tokenizer without retraining the vocabulary, this concern disappears for the text path. If they retrain the tokenizer (plausible for a specialized SaaS domain with jargon), it reappears.

Evidence: Redo preprocessing statistics using train-only data and measure whether evaluation metrics on the test set change meaningfully. If they don't, the leakage is benign; if they do, it's a real confound.

**Issue 3: Threshold transfer from validation to test after retraining on train+val**

The methodology sets the decision threshold on the validation set to achieve 80% recall, then applies that threshold to the test set after retraining on train+validation combined. After retraining on the larger dataset (train+val), the model's predicted probability distribution will shift — the model is no longer the same model that was calibrated on validation. If the score distribution shifts (typically scores concentrate closer to 0 and 1 as training set grows), the threshold that achieved 80% recall on validation will yield a different recall on test. This is not just a minor perturbation: if the test recall drifts to, say, 75% or 85%, the precision-at-80%-recall metric is no longer being measured — instead, precision at an arbitrary recall level is being reported as the primary metric, which undermines the entire evaluation design.

This is not a precision-recall tradeoff concern; it is a measurement validity concern. The evaluation purports to measure a specific operating point but may not be measuring that point.

Evidence: Report the actual achieved recall on the test set alongside the reported precision. If test recall is not within ±1–2% of 80%, the primary metric is being mismeasured. A correct approach is to re-derive the threshold directly on a held-out subset of the combined train+val set or to compute precision at the actual achieved recall on the test set and acknowledge the operating point shift.

**Summary of verdict:**

Issue 1 is the most fundamental — it conflates interpolation performance with extrapolation performance, which is the actual deployment task, and it differentially favors the transformer whose text representations are most sensitive to temporal lexical drift. Issue 3 is a concrete measurement validity problem with the stated primary metric. Issue 2 is conditional on implementation choices but worth verifying. These are not manufactured concerns; each has a specific failure mechanism.

issues_raised: Stratified random split across time creates soft temporal leakage because a customer's later observations appear in training while earlier ones appear in test, contaminating learned representations with future customer trajectories.

Preprocessing statistics (scaling, vocabulary, imputation) derived from the full corpus including test data constitutes leakage for evolving support language distributions, even though no labels are used.

The threshold fixed on the validation set to achieve 80% recall will yield a different actual recall on the test set, making the precision-at-80%-recall comparison between models invalid if effective recall levels differ.

The experiment conflates 'does text contain signal beyond tabular features' with 'does the transformer architecture outperform GBT,' requiring a 2x2 ablation (text-only vs. tabular-only vs. combined, for both architectures) to separate these questions.
Auditing only 100 transcripts for post-churn text contamination is statistically underpowered to detect rare but systematic filtering failures in the churned subpopulation.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
The experiment is well-structured overall, but I can identify several genuine methodological issues worth examining.

**Issue 1: Stratified random split instead of temporal split — the core leakage risk**

The methodology uses stratified sampling over prediction months so each split contains comparable month-level composition. This is presented as a strength (robustness to unusual quarters), but it is actually a form of temporal leakage. In a production setting, the model will always predict the future from the past. When train/val/test are constructed by randomly sampling prediction points across the full 18–36 month window, a customer's month-12 observation can appear in training while their month-6 observation appears in test. This means the model observes (implicitly, through shared customer history) future information about customer behavior during training.

More concretely: customer A appears in the training set at month 18 (labeled churn=1). Customer A's month-6 observation appears in the test set (labeled churn=0). The month-6 feature window includes events before day N for month 6, so there's no direct feature leakage. However, the model has learned the behavioral signature of customer A at month 18, which shares significant distributional overlap with month 6. Across 50,000+ customer-months, this creates a systematic soft leakage where the model's learned representations are contaminated by future customer trajectories.

The correct split is a clean temporal cutoff: all prediction points before date T_1 are training, T_1 to T_2 are validation, T_2 onward are test. The stated goal of avoiding dependence on a single unusual quarter is better addressed by ensuring the temporal test window is long enough to span multiple quarters, not by mixing time periods across splits.

Evidence that would resolve this: compare AUC-ROC under random stratified split vs. strict temporal split. If the stratified split inflates performance by 2–5 AUC points, this is exactly the contamination mechanism. This kind of gap is regularly observed in practice with customer churn datasets.

**Issue 2: Preprocessing frozen on full-dataset corpus statistics — a subtle leakage**

The methodology states: 'Preprocessing components that do not use labels will be parameterized once using corpus-level statistics from the full extracted feature pool.' This means scaling parameters (mean, variance), tokenization vocabulary, and imputation statistics are derived from the entire dataset including the test set.

For tabular scaling, this is minor — mean/variance leakage from test is small if the test set is 15% of a large corpus. But for tokenization vocabulary construction and unsupervised imputation, the contamination can be more significant. If the vocabulary is built from all support chat transcripts including those in the test window, the transformer's embedding space has implicitly seen test-period language. For a SaaS product where support language evolves over time (new feature names, updated pricing terminology, product pivots), vocabulary exposure from the test period is genuine signal leakage.

The correct procedure is to derive all preprocessing statistics from the training set only, then apply them to validation and test. The methodology explicitly forbids label-dependent transformations on non-training data but permits unsupervised transformations on the full corpus — this distinction is not as safe as it appears.

Evidence: build vocabulary from train only vs. full corpus, measure OOV rate on test transcripts under train-only vocabulary. A high OOV rate (>5–10%) would indicate the vocabulary was doing real work, making the leakage consequential.

**Issue 3: Threshold-setting procedure introduces optimistic precision-at-recall estimates**

The threshold is set on the validation set to achieve 80% recall, then this fixed threshold is applied to the test set. The problem is that 'precision-at-80%-recall' is a discrete operation that depends on the threshold achieving exactly 80% recall on the test set — which it will not, because the score distribution on test differs from validation.

In practice, fixing the threshold from validation and evaluating on test will yield a recall that is not exactly 80%. The reported 'precision-at-80%-recall' is therefore actually 'precision at [some recall close to 80%] that was targeted from validation.' This becomes a comparison problem: if the transformer's validation-set threshold happens to yield 78% recall on test while the baseline's threshold yields 82% recall on test, the precision values are not comparable at the same operating point.

The correct procedure is either: (a) report precision at the exact recall achieved on test (not the targeted recall), making clear the operating points differ; (b) interpolate the precision-recall curve on the test set to evaluate at exactly 80% recall for both models; or (c) use AUC-PR (area under the precision-recall curve) as the primary metric, which avoids threshold sensitivity entirely.

This is not a minor nit — the entire primary success criterion is built on this metric, and the comparison between models is invalid if the two models are evaluated at different effective recall levels.

Evidence: report the actual recall achieved on the test set for each model under its validation-derived threshold. If the two models differ by more than 2 percentage points in actual test recall, the precision comparison is confounded.

**Issue 4: Transformer prerequisite assumption — text contains signal not already encoded in tabular features**

The hypothesis is that transformer text signal beats a well-tuned tree model on structured features. But the tabular features already include support-ticket count (90d), payment-failure count (90d), plan-downgrade flags, and login recency. These are strong proxies for the same intent signals that support chat text would carry. If a customer has 5 support tickets in 90 days and logged in 0 times in 30 days, the text of those support tickets adds marginal information.

This is not a claim that text adds zero signal — it clearly can. But the experiment's framing implicitly assumes text signal is not already largely captured by its own metadata (ticket count, recency). The relevant ablation is: transformer trained on text + tabular vs. transformer trained on tabular only (i.e., does text add to tabular even for the transformer?). Without this, a null result (transformer ≈ baseline) cannot be interpreted: it could mean text adds nothing, or it could mean text adds something but the transformer fails to extract it beyond what its tabular head already learned.

The design as written conflates 'does text contain signal' with 'does the transformer extract that signal better than GBT extracts tabular signal.' These are two separate questions and the experiment only answers the compound question.

Evidence: include a third model — BERT fine-tuned on text only, no tabular features — and a fourth — GBT on tabular only with an added text-embedding feature (e.g., from a frozen encoder). This 2x2 design disambiguates the modality question from the architecture question.

**Issue 5: Manual audit of 100 transcripts is underpowered for post-churn text contamination**

The methodology audits 100 random transcripts from the test set to verify no cancellation signal leaks through. With a 15% test set of 50,000+ customer-months (7,500+ examples), and churn prevalence likely in the 5–20% range (375–1,500 churned customers in test), an audit of 100 transcripts has low probability of catching a systematic but rare contamination pattern — e.g., if 2% of churned accounts have a subtle post-churn signal (agent-flagged-but-not-keyword-matching cancellation summaries). This is a quality control measure with insufficient statistical power to provide confidence.

This is a lesser concern than issues 1–4 because the filtering procedure is described (agent-marked cancellation requests, closure summaries excluded, 2-day post-observation buffer), but the audit is not a reliable backstop if the filtering rule has edge cases.

Evidence: expand audit to all churned accounts in the test set (not a random sample), and specifically audit accounts where churn occurred within 3–7 days after the prediction point, as these are most likely to have chat activity in the ambiguous zone.
